head_coach.name: Matt LaFleur
head_coach.scheme_philosophy: Shanahan/McVay wide zone system with play action emphasis; LaFleur calls all offensive plays. Condensed formations, RPO elements, and a mobile QB dimension under Jordan Love.

offensive_coordinator.name: Adam Stenavich
offensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Shanahan/LaFleur wide zone (SF 49ers assistant OL coach under Shanahan 2017-18, then LaFleur disciple since 2019). OL/run game specialist background.

defensive_coordinator.name: Jonathan Gannon
defensive_coordinator.scheme_tree: Zimmer/Eberflus/Fangio hybrid. Learned under Mike Zimmer in Minnesota (2014-17), Matt Eberflus in Indianapolis (2018-20), then ran Fangio-adjacent system as Eagles DC (2021-22) and Cardinals HC (2023-25). Extremely adaptable — uses both 3-4 and 4-3 fronts, heavy quarters coverage, disguised pressures.

offense.personnel_grouping_rates: 67% 11 personnel, 25% 12 personnel, 6% 21 personnel (2024 data via SumerSports; expected similar split in 2025-26)
offense.run_scheme: Wide zone base with increasing gap/power concepts. LaFleur has added more pulling linemen and gap scheme runs in recent seasons, moving toward a 'power spread' approach with heavier OL. Play action and boot concepts remain foundational.
offense.pass_concepts: Play action heavy, bootleg/rollout concepts, condensed formation designs that generate explosive passing plays. Vertical shots off play action. 16.5% of passes went 15+ yards in 2024 (league leader tier).
offense.te_usage: Primary receiving weapon when healthy. Tucker Kraft is an elite move TE used flexed, in slot, and as a YAC machine (led all TEs in YAC/reception at 10.8 in 2025). Also used as inline blocker in 12 personnel. Kraft suffered torn ACL in Week 9 of 2025 — his return is the biggest offensive variable for 2026.
offense.rb_usage: Bell cow model with Josh Jacobs. Primary runner with moderate receiving role (36 catches, 282 receiving yards in 2025). PFF graded Jacobs 90.0 as a receiver in 2025 (3rd among HBs). Run game struggled in 2025 despite heavy investment in OL size.

defense.base_front: hybrid (3-4 base principles with frequent 4-down nickel looks)
defense.alignment: Multiple front. GM Gutekunst hinted at 2026 Combine that Gannon will incorporate more '3-4 principles in base' than under Hafley, but acknowledged 'it's a nickel defense league' and predicted frequent four-down fronts with 5 DBs. Gannon ran 3-4 in Arizona and 4-3 in Philadelphia — expects to bounce between both.
defense.edge_rush_source: Hybrid. Micah Parsons is the primary pass rusher — played 85% of snaps off the edge in 2025 as a 4-3 DE but projects as a stand-up OLB in 3-4 looks. Lukas Van Ness on the opposite edge. Gannon's scheme in Arizona used OLBs (Zaven Collins, Dennis Gardeck) as primary edge rushers in 3-4 sets. Parsons' versatility to rush from DE, OLB, and even interior makes him the perfect fit for Gannon's multiple fronts.
defense.coverage_base: Quarters (Cover 4) heavy with Cover 3 and Cover 1 mixed in. In Arizona, Gannon led the entire NFL in Cover 4 usage in 2025. Also runs significant man coverage when matchups dictate. Emphasizes disguised coverages and pre-snap confusion.
defense.blitz_tendency: low-to-moderate (Gannon's defenses historically rank low in actual blitz rate but use heavy simulated pressures and disguised fronts. Under Hafley in 2024-25, Packers blitzed ~20-27% of the time. Gannon prefers generating pressure with the front four while dropping into zone.)
defense.lb_role: ILBs (Edgerrin Cooper, Quay Walker) are run/coverage hybrid players. In Gannon's 3-4 looks, OLBs become primary pass rushers. Cooper is an ascending player who took over as a starter in 2024. Under Gannon, expect more variety in LB usage — some OLB blitzing, some dropping into coverage.
defense.safety_usage: Three-safety looks are a hallmark. Xavier McKinney (elite free safety, 2x All-Pro), Evan Williams (strong safety), and Javon Bullard (slot/nickel hybrid who also plays safety). Gannon historically favors three-safety personnel — in Arizona he used 'a slew of combinations in the back seven' with three-safety looks allowing rotation into blitzing, coverage, and box roles. Bullard's versatility as a slot defender who can also play box safety is ideal for Gannon.

te_receiving_boost.explanation: Tucker Kraft's usage proves Green Bay values elite receiving TEs at near-WR levels. Before his torn ACL in Week 9 of 2025, Kraft was the team's top receiver (32 catches, 489 yards, 6 TDs in 8 games), leading all TEs in yards per reception and YAC per reception. LaFleur's offense features the TE in condensed formations, flexed to the slot, and as the primary play-action target. The Packers ran 25% 12 personnel, and Kraft was on pace for a potential All-Pro season. Any TE prospect with elite receiving traits (route running, hands, YAC, speed) gets a significant value boost for Green Bay, especially with Kraft recovering from his ACL and Luke Musgrave proving unreliable as a backup.

lb_pass_rush_boost.explanation: Jonathan Gannon's hiring fundamentally changes this calculus. Gannon ran a 3-4 in Arizona where OLBs (Zaven Collins, Dennis Gardeck, BJ Ojulari) were the primary edge rushers. He split his defensive front coaching in Arizona into a '3-4 DL coach and a 3-4 outside linebackers coach.' Micah Parsons himself is transitioning from a 4-3 DE to what projects as a 3-4 OLB — the same archetype he played early in his career in Dallas. With Rashan Gary likely departing, Green Bay needs a second edge presence opposite Parsons. Any LB prospect with pass rush traits (burst, bend, power moves) who can stand up and rush from the OLB spot in a 3-4 gets a major value boost. Gannon's Eagles had four players with 10+ sacks in 2022 — he knows how to maximize edge talent in multiple alignments.

rb_dual_threat_boost.explanation: LaFleur's Shanahan-derived offense does value receiving backs, and Josh Jacobs showed elite receiving ability in 2025 (PFF 90.0 receiving grade, 3rd among HBs). However, Green Bay primarily uses Jacobs as a bell-cow runner — his receiving role is complementary, not primary. The offense is built around play action and the run game first, with RB targets being checkdowns and screens rather than designed routes. A dual-threat RB gets a moderate boost, but Green Bay won't draft an RB high purely for receiving ability.

s_hybrid_boost.explanation: Gannon's scheme is built for hybrid safeties. In Arizona, he favored three-safety looks with safeties rotating between blitzing, coverage, and box roles. The Packers already have the prototype: Javon Bullard plays slot corner (493 snaps) and safety (218 snaps) interchangeably. McKinney is the elite deep FS, Williams is the strong safety, and Bullard is the Swiss Army knife. Gannon's use of multiple safeties to confuse opposing QBs means any safety prospect with versatility — who can play in the box, cover the slot, blitz, and also align deep — gets a significant value boost. The Packers' defensive identity under Gannon will be defined by their ability to deploy three safeties in multiple disguised looks.

scheme_specific_draft_notes: The Gannon hire is the biggest variable for Green Bay's 2026 draft. His 3-4/multiple front system creates immediate demand for a stand-up OLB/EDGE hybrid opposite Parsons (Rashan Gary likely gone), making pass-rushing LBs like Arvell Reese or Sonny Styles significantly more valuable to GB than to traditional 4-3 teams. On defense, Gannon's quarters-heavy coverage and three-safety personnel mean versatile safeties and big slot corners who can rotate roles get a premium. Cornerback is a critical need after losing Jaire Alexander and Eric Stokes. On offense, TE is a sneaky need — Tucker Kraft's ACL recovery timeline is uncertain, and the offense collapsed without him. A receiving TE with YAC ability would be valued near WR1 prices. The OL also needs attention after a brutal 2025, particularly at center. LaFleur still calls plays, and the offense will remain Shanahan-rooted regardless of any OC changes. Gannon's adaptable defensive philosophy means Green Bay will value versatile defensive prospects who can play multiple positions over rigid specialists.
sources: ESPN (Rob Demovsky reporting on Gannon hire, Parsons trade analysis)
SI / Packers On SI (Bill Huber's Combine reporting on Gannon 3-4/4-3 scheme)
Acme Packing Company (offensive approach analysis, defensive scheme breakdowns, Gannon coaching tree)
A to Z Sports (Gannon scheme analysis, Stenavich coaching tree)
